title: The 10/90 Rule
subtitle: Do 10% of users really get 90% of the likes?
claim: 10% of dating app users receive 90% of all likes.
explanation: The reality follows a power-law distribution, but the numbers are not quite as dramatic as the meme suggests. Our data shows the top 10% of profiles receive roughly 58% of all likes. Still wildly unequal, but the viral 90% figure is an exaggeration born from a single OkCupid blog post in 2009. The inequality is real; the specific ratio is not.

title: The Catfish Effect
subtitle: Are people less attractive in real life than their photos?
claim: People are generally less attractive in real life than they appear in their dating profile photos.
explanation: Surprise: most people actually look about the same or better in person. In a study where participants rated attractiveness before and after meeting, 62% of dates were rated as attractive or more attractive than expected. The dreaded let-down? It happens just 17% of the time. The remaining 21% landed exactly on expectations. Our brains are just wired to expect the worst.

title: The Height Hurdle
subtitle: Does height actually matter on dating apps?
claim: Height is a major factor in dating app success, especially for men.
explanation: The numbers do not lie, and they are not kind. Men who list their height as 6'0" (183 cm) or above receive 32% more likes than men under 5'9" (175 cm). But before short kings despair: profiles with humor and personality-forward bios close 60% of that gap. Height opens the door, but charm walks through it.
